    "opinion": "I am a retired woman, living in Milan, who enjoys birdwatching and rock climbing. Social media is not something I spend much time on, only occasionally when my children remind me of important events or family gatherings. But even from these brief encounters, I can see how fake content spreads easily online.\n\nGrowing up native-born Italian with a primary school education, but having parents who were educated in different ways – one went to lower secondary and the other has a PhD – gives me a bit of perspective on information being presented as truth. My mother only finished lower secondary school, so she's not an expert on social media trends or algorithms. Yet, my father with his PhD is knowledgeable about facts and verifying authenticity.\n\nI must say, seeing misinformation go viral makes me feel quite uncomfortable. It reminds me when I was young during some political events that weren't what they seemed at first glance. With a high neuroticism score, I worry deeply about the truthfulness of things shared around me – whether it's fake news or misleading content.\n\nAs someone with low openness but moderate agreeableness, I believe in treating everyone with kindness and respect, including those who might share false information without knowing it. However, dealing with this requires being cautious and perhaps asking trusted family members like my father for help when verifying something unusual. My Islamic faith also teaches me to be wary of spreading untruths, as honesty is paramount.\n\nOne thing I do know from my life experience: if you're unsure about the accuracy of a piece of information online, it's better not to jump on that bandwagon without checking first. And with my low extraversion and moderate conscientiousness, I prefer quiet reflection over quick sharing anyway.",

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
I am a retired woman, living in Milan, who enjoys birdwatching and rock climbing. Social media is not something I spend much time on, only occasionally when my children remind me of important events or family gatherings. But even from these brief encounters, I can see how fake content spreads easily online.

Growing up native-born Italian with a primary school education, but having parents who were educated in different ways – one went to lower secondary and the other has a PhD – gives me a bit of perspective on information being presented as truth. My mother only finished lower secondary school, so she's not an expert on social media trends or algorithms. Yet, my father with his PhD is knowledgeable about facts and verifying authenticity.

I must say, seeing misinformation go viral makes me feel quite uncomfortable. It reminds me when I was young during some political events that weren't what they seemed at first glance. With a high neuroticism score, I worry deeply about the truthfulness of things shared around me – whether it's fake news or misleading content.

As someone with low openness but moderate agreeableness, I believe in treating everyone with kindness and respect, including those who might share false information without knowing it. However, dealing with this requires being cautious and perhaps asking trusted family members like my father for help when verifying something unusual. My Islamic faith also teaches me to be wary of spreading untruths, as honesty is paramount.

One thing I do know from my life experience: if you're unsure about the accuracy of a piece of information online, it's better not to jump on that bandwagon without checking first. And with my low extraversion and moderate conscientiousness, I prefer quiet reflection over quick sharing anyway.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: I have lived through historical times where misinformation caused confusion; as a retired person with limited social media use but occasional exposure (low openness), I rely on critical thinking and verification from trusted sources like my father. My high neuroticism makes me sensitive to false content, while Islam emphasizes honesty.
